metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test node to AC4 (e.g. 'verified by ::test_lease_expiry_reclaimable') and replace 're-processing is idempotent' with a specific observable outcome (e.g. 'a second CLAIM_ENRICH lands for the same ticket after lease expiry, and exactly one DONE_ENRICH is appended').
findings.finding: AC4 names no proving command or test node, leaving lease reclaimability and idempotency unverifiable in-session; 're-processing is idempotent' is property-framed without a specific observable outcome.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] A claim with `lease_expires_ns < now` is reclaimable on the next drain (self-healing reaper); re-processing is idempotent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command to AC5, e.g. 'verified by `grep -E "ENQUEUE_ENRICH|CLAIM_ENRICH|DONE_ENRICH" src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` for type registration, and `::test_prune_bounds_queue_events` asserting event count per ticket equals N after prune.'
findings.finding: AC5 asserts 'queue-event growth is bounded by the named prune path' but provides no proving command — no grep, no pytest node, no artifact assertion — making it unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] The three event types are in `EVENT_TYPES` and `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`; queue-event growth is bounded by the named prune path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the hardcoded event-type error string in `stage_and_commit` (src/rebar/_store/event_append.py lines 97-103) to include ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, and DONE_ENRICH, or replace the literal with a dynamic join over `EVENT_TYPES`.
findings.finding: stage_and_commit contains a hardcoded error-message string (lines 97-103 of event_append.py) that explicitly enumerates all valid event types. Adding ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, and DONE_ENRICH to the EVENT_TYPES frozenset will NOT update this string automatically, leaving it stale and misleading for operators who see the error.
findings.location: src/rebar/_store/event_append.py lines 97-103 / Scope section: 'EVENT_TYPES: add the three types'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the hardcoded string with a dynamic join: `f"Error: invalid event_type '{event_type}'. Must be one of: {', '.join(sorted(EVENT_TYPES))}"` — this keeps the error message always in sync with the frozenset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the locked read-check-append is implemented given that `stage_and_commit` acquires `write_lock` internally: either add a new `_locked_claim_append(tracker, ticket_id, event, lock_handle)` primitive to `event_append.py` that accepts an already-held `LockHandle` and skips the internal lock acquisition, or describe an equivalent split-lock pattern.
findings.finding: The plan's claim critical section ('read-check-then-append INSIDE the write_lock') cannot be directly implemented using the existing `stage_and_commit` primitive, which acquires `write_lock` internally. On Linux, `fcntl.flock` is per-open-file-description: a second fd from the same process attempting LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB on the same inode while the first fd already holds LOCK_EX will fail (NB) or deadlock (blocking), so wrapping a `stage_and_commit` call inside an outer `write_lock` context would deadlock or have the inner lock acquisition fail. The plan provides no guidance on how to implement the locked-reduce-then-append as a single atomic operation given this constraint; a new primitive (e.g. `_locked_claim_append` in `event_append.py` that accepts an already-held `LockHandle`, or an internal `_stage_and_commit_locked` variant that skips the internal lock acquisition) is required.
findings.location: ## Claim correctness section; `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` `stage_and_commit` (line 121: `with _lock.write_lock(...)`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_stage_and_commit_under_lock(tracker, ticket_id, event, lock_handle)` internal function to `event_append.py` that performs the rename+git-add+git-commit without re-acquiring the lock, and expose `lock.acquire()` → `LockHandle` as the external lock primitive. The claim path then: `handle = lock.acquire(tracker); try: state = reduce(ticket_id); if claimable: _stage_and_commit_under_lock(..., handle); finally: handle.release()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config-placement contradiction: specify unambiguously which dataclass and file each new config key (SOAK, LEASE_TTL, overlap_enabled) belongs to, and name the exact env vars and config-file keys for each, matching the resolution pattern in LLMConfig.from_env.
findings.finding: The config placement for SOAK and LEASE_TTL is internally contradictory: the plan says they go into 'the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)' but no 'overlap' section exists in _config_schema.py (verified: registered sections are verify/ticket/ticket_clarity/compact/sync/mcp/reconciler/jira/scratch/tracker). The plan simultaneously says they are 'new overlap_* keys on LLMConfig (src/rebar/llm/config.py)' — two different files/classes — and that overlap_enabled lives in VerifyConfig [verify]. An implementor cannot resolve which class and which config file each new key belongs to.
findings.location: ## Scope — Config: 'SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the prune path to a first-class scope item: name the target function/file, specify the `keep=N` value, add an edit target in the Scope section, and add an AC (with a pytest target) verifying that after N re-cert cycles only the latest ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE triple is retained.
findings.finding: The retirement/prune path for accumulated ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH events is mentioned only in a prose note with no AC, no named function, and no retention count specified — leaving the 'growth bounded' claim unverifiable and the implementation target ambiguous.
findings.location: 'Scope' section — 'Log-growth note'; AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the three new event types (`ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, `DONE_ENRICH`) to the hard-coded error message string in `stage_and_commit` (lines 97-103 of `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`) as an explicit edit target in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `stage_and_commit` in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` contains a hard-coded error message string (lines 97-103) that enumerates ALL valid event type names as a prose literal. The plan's Scope says to add the three new types to `EVENT_TYPES`, but this is insufficient: if the error-message string is not also updated to include `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, and `DONE_ENRICH`, any attempt to append these types will be rejected by the guard at line 96 with an error message that does not name them, making debugging extremely confusing. The plan excludes this edit target invisibly.
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` `EVENT_TYPES`: add the three types'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for event_append.py, add: 'also update the hard-coded error message string in `stage_and_commit` (lines 97-103) to include all three new type names.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `test_claim_race` exercises the real `write_lock` / `fcntl.flock` (e.g. via a real temp-file store fixture, not a mock), to ensure the mutual-exclusion invariant is actually verified rather than assumed.
findings.finding: The plan's most critical correctness guarantee — 'exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH lands' — rests entirely on the real OS-level `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` write lock. The `test_claim_race` AC is in `tests/unit/` and the plan never explicitly states that the test uses the real lock (versus a mock/stub of `write_lock`). If the unit test mocks the store's write lock, it proves nothing about the actual exclusivity guarantee and constitutes proxy-validation of the claim's central invariant.
findings.location: Testing section / AC `::test_claim_race` / 'Claim correctness' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Testing section: 'test_claim_race uses a real on-disk store fixture (temp directory) so that the actual fcntl.flock LOCK_EX boundary is exercised; no mock of write_lock is permitted in this test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a corresponding named test, e.g. `test_implicit_enqueue_fallback`) that verifies: a ticket that is certified but has no `ENQUEUE_ENRICH` event is returned by `pending_enrichment` after the soak period, proving the crash self-healing path is implemented.
findings.finding: The crash-between-cert-and-ENQUEUE self-healing property is stated as a key correctness claim but has no acceptance criterion or test covering it.
findings.location: ## What section: 'a crash between the cert and the ENQUEUE append is self-healed (nothing is permanently missed)'; ## Testing section: no test for self-healing path; ## Acceptance Criteria: no AC covers it
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] A ticket certified with no subsequent ENQUEUE_ENRICH event (simulating a crash between cert and enqueue) is returned by pending_enrichment(now) after soak; verified by ::test_implicit_enqueue_fallback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC (with a mock drain that exercises the 'certified-but-no-ENQUEUE' detection path) to make the crash-recovery claim verifiable in this story, or explicitly scope it as a deferred guarantee in S4's acceptance criteria with a clear risk note here.
findings.finding: The crash-between-cert-and-ENQUEUE self-healing mechanism is a stated correctness claim but has no AC or test, and the drain component it relies on (S4) is explicitly out of scope for this story, making the claim unverifiable within this delivery.
findings.location: 'What' section — 'Enqueue is a SEPARATE append...' paragraph; 'Dependencies' and 'OUT: the drain runner (S4)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` analogy in the Scope section: replace 'like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST' with 'like REVIEW_RESULT' (the only current precedent), and add an explicit note that TICKET_DIGEST is added by S2 and must be landed before or alongside this story.
findings.finding: The plan describes the three new event types as 'sidecar, preserved-and-ignored; like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST', treating TICKET_DIGEST as a co-existing member of `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` and `EVENT_TYPES`. But at current HEAD, TICKET_DIGEST is absent from both sets — it is the deliverable of the S2 dependency (wispy-cheerless-tick), not yet landed. The assertion that TICKET_DIGEST is already an analogous precedent in these sets is false as of the current codebase.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add them to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (sidecar, preserved-and-ignored; like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'sidecar, preserved-and-ignored; like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST' to 'sidecar, preserved-and-ignored; like REVIEW_RESULT (TICKET_DIGEST is added by S2 and must land first)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append on the cert path: define whether a write failure raises (blocking cert), is logged-and-swallowed (graceful degradation), or triggers a retry — and ensure failures are observable via a log line or metric.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append that fires on the certification hot path. If the event-store write fails (disk full, lock contention timeout, I/O error), it is unspecified whether certification itself fails, the error is logged and swallowed, or the caller is notified — leaving the enqueue silently missing.
findings.location: ## What — 'Enqueue is a SEPARATE append immediately after certification'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the enqueue append in a try/except; on failure, log a WARNING with ticket_id and exception detail (so the drain's self-heal can be correlated), and let certification succeed — making enqueue a best-effort side-effect consistent with the 'correctness does not rely on atomicity' statement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append hook: state whether StoreError/LockTimeout should be swallowed (best-effort, like REVIEW_RESULT) or propagated, and add a corresponding AC/test for the crash-recovery/self-healing path if swallowed.
findings.finding: The enqueue hook's error-handling fallback is unspecified. The plan says the enqueue is 'a SEPARATE append immediately after certification' and that a crash between cert and enqueue is self-healed, but does not specify whether a StoreError/LockTimeout on the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append should be swallowed (like REVIEW_RESULT sidecar) or propagated to the caller. This is a load-bearing decision: propagating it makes certification fail on a transient git-lock race; swallowing it requires the self-healing path to be tested.
findings.location: ## What — 'Enqueue is a SEPARATE append immediately after certification'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror sidecar.py: swallow all exceptions from the enqueue append with logger.warning, and add an AC: 'A StoreError on the ENQUEUE append is logged and does not fail certification; the self-healing path is verified by ::test_enqueue_failure_self_heals'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'prune(keep=…)' with the concrete retention value (e.g. 'keep=1 per ticket per cycle, mirroring sidecar.py's keep=1 behavior') so AC5 is measurable and the executor has no decision to make.
findings.finding: The prune retention quantity is left as an ellipsis placeholder ('keep=…'), deferring the retention decision to the executor rather than stating a default.
findings.location: ## Scope section: 'a `prune(keep=…)` retention (mirroring `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`) keeps only a ticket's latest ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE cycle'
findings.suggested_fix: State 'prune(keep=1) per ticket (retain only the latest ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE triple, matching sidecar.py)' and update AC5 to assert the event count for a ticket after prune equals the expected N.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the Scope or Dependencies section that S3's edits to EVENT_TYPES and _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES must be coordinated with S2's additions to the same frozensets, and clarify the required landing order (S2 before S3).
findings.finding: Both this plan (S3) and the S2 dependency (wispy-cheerless-tick) add new event types to the SAME two files: src/rebar/_store/event_append.py (EVENT_TYPES) and src/rebar/reducer/_version.py (_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES). S2 adds TICKET_DIGEST; S3 adds ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, DONE_ENRICH. The plan does not acknowledge or address the merge/ordering dependency on those shared files.
findings.location: Scope section / Dependencies section: 'S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the queue-event prune (mirroring sidecar.py) is safe to run outside the write lock, or specify that it must run under `write_lock` (unlike REVIEW_RESULT which is observability-only and genuinely safe to prune without the lock).
findings.finding: The plan asserts the queue-event prune 'mirrors src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py' — but sidecar.py's prune() runs two git commands (git rm + git commit) via subprocess.run OUTSIDE the write lock. For REVIEW_RESULT this is safe because those events are observability-only and not operationally load-bearing. For ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE queue-state events, a prune commit running outside the write lock can race with a concurrent CLAIM_ENRICH or ENQUEUE_ENRICH append, producing a git commit ordering where a live queue-state event is deleted after it was appended but before it was acted on. The plan does not verify that this race is harmless or justify why the prune is safe without the write lock.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Log-growth note: these sidecar events accumulate like REVIEW_RESULT; the drain appends DONE_ENRICH and a prune(keep=…) retention (mirroring src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) run the queue-event prune inside write_lock (unlike sidecar.py) with a single stage_and_commit for each pruned file, accepting the per-file commit overhead; or (b) scope the prune to delete only events that belong to a COMPLETED cycle (i.e., a DONE_ENRICH is present and confirmed committed) and document in the plan that the race with a re-ENQUEUE is safe because a new ENQUEUE_ENRICH will have a strictly later HLC timestamp and the drain's implicit-enqueue self-heal path covers the gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Bound the claim's write-lock hold time: either pre-compute the queue state outside the lock and re-check only the claim entry under the lock, or document a max event-log size assumption and add a lock-acquisition timeout so the drainer fails fast rather than blocking indefinitely.
findings.finding: The read-check-then-append claim holds `write_lock` (an exclusive `fcntl.flock`) across a reducer call (re-reducing the ticket's queue state from events). If the reducer is slow or the event log is large, this lock is held for an extended period, blocking ALL other store writes (including unrelated cert appends) for that duration. No timeout or bounded-hold strategy is mentioned.
findings.location: ## Claim correctness — 'while holding write_lock, re-reduce the ticket's queue state'
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-reduce queue state for the candidate ticket OUTSIDE the write_lock (read-only); acquire write_lock; re-read only the last CLAIM/DONE event for that ticket (O(1) tail read) to confirm still claimable; append CLAIM_ENRICH; release. This minimises lock hold time to a single tail-read + append.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `cert_time` explicitly in the 'What' section: state whether `not_before_ns` is computed as `hlc.next_tick(tracker, ticket_id) + SOAK_NS` (i.e., the ENQUEUE event's own HLC tick plus soak) or as `signature_event_timestamp + SOAK_NS` (the prior SIGNATURE event's tick plus soak), and align the AC test assertion accordingly.
findings.finding: The formula `not_before_ns = cert_time + SOAK` uses the term `cert_time` without defining whether this is (a) the HLC tick of the SIGNATURE event (obtained from the signing path), (b) the HLC tick generated by calling `next_tick` at ENQUEUE_ENRICH append time, or (c) `physical_now()` at enqueue time. Since the ENQUEUE append happens after the SIGNATURE commit (separate stage_and_commit), these timestamps differ; if the reducer inspects `not_before_ns` relative to the HLC tick of the ENQUEUE_ENRICH event itself rather than an independent wall-clock base, the soak window will be slightly different than intended. The ambiguity also affects the test AC: `test_enqueue_and_recert` verifies `not_before_ns = cert + SOAK` but 'cert' is undefined.
findings.location: ## What section — 'not_before_ns = cert_time + SOAK (SOAK default 60 min, configurable)'; ## Testing AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL` belong in `LLMConfig` (`[tool.rebar.llm]`) rather than the core typed Config (e.g. a `[verify]` extension or a new `[enrich]` section), given that all existing event-store timing tunables (`compact.COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS`, `reconciler.lock_lease_secs`, `verify.remediation_window_minutes`) live in the core config and SOAK/LEASE_TTL are not LLM prompt/scoring parameters.
findings.finding: SOAK and LEASE_TTL are placed in `LLMConfig` ([tool.rebar.llm]) but every existing analog for event-store timing mechanics lives in the core typed Config, making this config-surface proliferation across two sections with wrong-layer placement.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL` in the typed `overlap` config section… new `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig`… Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig`'
findings.suggested_fix: Move `overlap_soak_minutes` and `overlap_lease_ttl_minutes` into `VerifyConfig` (alongside `remediation_window_minutes`, which is the direct analog) or a new `[enrich]` core section, keeping only the enable flag (`overlap_enabled`) there as well. This consolidates the entire feature's config surface into one section and matches the pattern every existing analog follows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename 'optimistic claim' in the title to 'lock-guarded claim' (or 'serialized claim') to match the body's explicit 'READ-CHECK-THEN-APPEND INSIDE the write lock' design.
findings.finding: Title says 'optimistic claim' but the body explicitly states 'NOT lock-free CAS' and uses an EXCLUSIVE write lock for the read-check-then-append; 'optimistic' typically implies CAS / no lock, contradicting the implementation.
findings.location: Title: 'optimistic claim' vs ## Claim correctness section: 'NOT lock-free CAS … READ-CHECK-THEN-APPEND INSIDE the write lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title suffix from 'optimistic claim' to 'lock-guarded claim'; the subtitle already has 'lock-guarded claim' — unify both to remove the contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC #5) that verifies SOAK and LEASE_TTL exist as `LLMConfig` fields with correct defaults (60 min / 15 min) and are overridable via `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SOAK_*` env vars, and that `overlap_enabled` is on `VerifyConfig`.
findings.finding: The plan describes concrete config deliverables — SOAK and LEASE_TTL as new keys on LLMConfig, and overlap_enabled on VerifyConfig — but no acceptance criterion verifies that these config keys exist, are placed in the correct config section, carry the stated defaults (60 min / 15 min), or are readable from the environment (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*). An implementer could place them in the wrong layer or use wrong names with no AC to catch it.
findings.location: Scope section ('Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section…') and AC list (no covering criterion)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #5: '…; `LLMConfig` exposes `overlap_soak_ns` (default 3600s) and `overlap_lease_ttl_ns` (default 900s) readable from `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SOAK_NS` / `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_LEASE_TTL_NS`; `VerifyConfig` exposes `overlap_enabled`; verified by `::test_config_defaults`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the event-sourced queue design decision, the HLC latest-wins supersession invariant, and the read-check-then-append claim contract, so the rationale survives beyond this ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a genuinely novel architectural pattern (event-sourced sidecar queue with HLC latest-wins reduce, file-lock-based atomic claim) but does not mention capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc. Future maintainers encountering the sidecar event types, the HLC supersession logic, or the read-check-then-append claim pattern will have no documented rationale.
findings.location: ## What / ## Claim correctness / ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-event-sourced-enrichment-queue.md` (or equivalent CLAUDE.md section) covering: (1) why event store rather than SQLite/broker, (2) how latest-wins is defined (HLC timestamp, not wall clock), (3) the lock contract that makes atomic claim correct, and (4) the self-healing crash-recovery invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least structured log lines (INFO/WARNING) for: successful enqueue, claim acquired/lost, lease-expired reap, and DONE_ENRICH — so the queue lifecycle is observable in logs without raw event inspection.
findings.finding: The plan introduces no observability for the new failure points it adds: failed or skipped enqueues, lease expirations/reaps, claim-race losers, and drain-time errors. Operators have no signal that the queue is healthy, stuck, or backing up.
findings.location: ## What, ## Claim correctness, ## Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the title/body contradiction: either update the document title to 'lock-guarded claim' (matching the H1 and body) or revise the implementation to a true optimistic approach (e.g., version-stamped CAS), and ensure AC wording matches the chosen model.
findings.finding: The plan's document title says 'optimistic claim' but the H1 heading and body explicitly implement a pessimistic lock-guarded claim (READ-CHECK-THEN-APPEND under exclusive write_lock). 'Optimistic' concurrency means attempt without locking and detect conflicts after the fact; the actual mechanism is the opposite.
findings.location: Plan title vs. H1 heading ('optimistic claim' vs. 'lock-guarded claim') and 'Claim correctness' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the document/story title to match the H1: 'Event-sourced enrichment queue: cert-triggered enqueue, 60-min soak, latest-wins, lock-guarded claim'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new source file that will house `pending_enrichment()`, the ENQUEUE_ENRICH emit helper, and the claim read-check-append logic (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py`), and add it to the Scope edit targets.
findings.finding: The plan names no concrete source file for the new enqueue/reducer/claim logic (the module inside src/rebar/llm/overlap/ or elsewhere that houses `pending_enrichment`, the claim read-check-append, and the ENQUEUE_ENRICH emit helper). The Scope section names the cert-path hook location and the config layer, but the primary new module is anonymous.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Enqueue hook in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path' and 'OUT: the drain runner (S4)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a canonical payload builder (or TypedDict) for each of the three new event bodies co-located with the new queue module, specifying exact field names, types, and an optional schema-version tag, mirroring sidecar.py::build_payload.
findings.finding: The body schemas for the three new event types (ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, DONE_ENRICH) are specified only as informal prose tuples and no canonical payload builder, TypedDict, or schema-version tag is defined. An implementor cannot determine exact field names, types, or which module owns payload construction — unlike REVIEW_RESULT which has sidecar.py::build_payload.
findings.location: ## What (event body descriptions)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the drainer_id format (e.g., UUID4 generated once per drain-run invocation, or hostname:pid), where it is generated, and add it to the canonical payload definition for CLAIM_ENRICH.
findings.finding: The `drainer_id` field in the CLAIM_ENRICH body is referenced throughout the plan (body schema, claim correctness section, lease protocol) but its format, source, and generation mechanism are never specified. An implementor cannot know whether it should be a UUID, hostname, PID, or process-unique string.
findings.location: ## What / ## Claim correctness — CLAIM_ENRICH body: {ticket_id, drainer_id, lease_expires_ns}
findings.suggested_fix: Use `str(uuid.uuid4())` generated once at drain-run startup, stored in a local variable and passed to every claim attempt in that run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the new queue-projection file path (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py`) to the Scope edit targets and list `pending_enrichment` there, matching the file-naming pattern used for digest_sidecar.py in S2.
findings.finding: The plan references a `pending_enrichment(now_ns)` projection but never specifies which module/file it lives in. Unlike S2's 'digest_sidecar.py', no new file is named for the queue reducer, leaving placement ambiguous for an implementor.
findings.location: ## What — 'Reducer projection pending_enrichment(now_ns)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py` to the Scope section: 'defines the three event-type constants, canonical payload builders, and pending_enrichment(tracker, ticket_id, now_ns) reducer'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
